title: Listening - Part 4: Narrating Audiobooks
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Daniel Ross, a professional audiobook narrator. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest is Daniel Ross, who records audiobooks for children and adults. Daniel, did you train as an actor? || Daniel: I acted in school plays, but I never imagined it as a career. At university I volunteered for a service producing recordings for people with sight loss. [24]Reading novels aloud for those listeners made me realise that using my voice to tell stories was work I genuinely wanted to pursue[/24]. After graduating, I made a short recording and sent it to audio publishers. || Presenter: What was difficult when you began recording professionally? || Daniel: I assumed doing amusing voices would be the challenging bit. In fact, inventing one is easy; remembering it is not. [25]If a character returns after fifty pages, I must make that person sound exactly as they did earlier in the book[/25]. I keep notes and brief samples for every main character now. || Presenter: How do you prepare for a book? || Daniel: I read it all before recording, even when the schedule is tight. I underline emotional moments, but there is another practical job. [26]Before entering the studio, I check the pronunciation of unfamiliar place names and people's names rather than guessing during a recording[/26]. A narrator loses authority quickly if a name keeps changing. || Presenter: Does a director tell you how to perform? || Daniel: A director may discuss the general style, but usually leaves the feelings to me. What they notice particularly is speed. [27]A director often asks me to slow down, since listeners need slightly more time to follow a story than readers looking at a printed page[/27]. Excitement can make me rush dialogue without noticing. || Presenter: You also record at home. Is that convenient? || Daniel: It saves travelling, and my little studio is comfortable. However, homes are not designed for silent work. [28]A delivery van, a neighbour cutting grass or even my washing machine can ruin a perfect paragraph and force me to begin it again[/28]. I have become unusually aware of every noise in my street. || Presenter: Do listeners recognise you? || Daniel: Rarely, and that is fine. Audiobook listeners create characters in their own imagination. [29]The nicest feedback is when somebody says my narration helped them enter the story, rather than commenting on me as a performer[/29]. The book should remain at the centre. || Presenter: What would you like to record next? || Daniel: Fiction is wonderful, but I listen to a lot of history programmes. [30]I would love to narrate a true story about an expedition, where the events are factual but the tension is as strong as in a novel[/30]. That combination would be an exciting new challenge.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What led Daniel to consider audiobook narration as a career?

options.A: Recording books for people unable to see printed text.
options.B: Being praised for his acting in a university play.
options.C: Listening to a professional recording of his favourite novel.

questions.explanation: He discovered his interest while volunteering to record books for listeners with sight loss.

questions.question: What did Daniel find difficult at the start of his career?

options.B: Keeping a character's voice consistent later in a book.

questions.explanation: He says characters must sound the same when they return many pages later.

questions.question: What preparation does Daniel do before going into the studio?

options.B: He learns about the author's life.
options.C: He checks how unfamiliar names should be said.

questions.explanation: He confirms the pronunciation of place and people's names rather than guessing.

questions.question: What do directors most often comment on?

options.A: The speed at which he is reading.
options.B: The amount of emotion he uses.
options.C: The voices he chooses for minor characters.

questions.explanation: Directors frequently ask him to slow down for listeners.

questions.question: What disadvantage of recording at home does Daniel mention?

options.A: His equipment is less reliable there.
options.B: Ordinary outside or household sounds interrupt him.
options.C: He finds it hard to work without a director.

questions.explanation: Vans, lawn cutting and appliances can spoil a paragraph and require another take.

questions.question: What kind of feedback does Daniel value most?

options.A: Being told his voice is easy to recognise.
options.B: Hearing that he made listeners focus on the story.

questions.explanation: He wants listeners to enter the story rather than concentrate on his performance.

questions.question: What type of audiobook would Daniel like to try?

options.A: A factual adventure with dramatic events.
options.B: A collection of poems for young readers.
options.C: A guide to improving acting skills.

questions.explanation: He says he would like to record a true expedition story with strong tension.
